Preface
This Manual has been developed as a guide to the internal Processes and procedures to
be followed by field formations of Inland Revenue in general and the Directorate Generai
of International Taxes FBR Pakistan in particular in respect of ail incoming and outgoing
Exchange of Information on Request (EOIR) and spontaneous Exchange of InJormation
(sEor).
This manual focuses on the sequential tasks that make uP the EoI process as weli as the
roles of those concerned with handling of EOIRs and SEOI. FBR is cognizant of the fact
that communications and clelivery of documents through postal services can add time
delays and, where possible, these can be reduced by use of eleckonic means for sending
inJoimation and by use of telephone and e-mail for checking on progress. The manual
also sets out brief responsibilities of all the positions involved in EOIRs and SEOIs, the
stantlards related process of information gathering to respond to the EolRs,
confidentiality of information exchanged.
The manual also outlines the salient features of EOI Database System placed at the Office
of secretary EoI, Directorate General of Intemationai Taxes, Federal Board of Revenue.
Moreover, the manual also provides checkiists for use by EoI staff and templates of
letters an<l statutory notices which are required to be used for both internal and external
correspondences of FBR for handling of EOIRs.
4

CHAPTER-I EOI WORK MANUAL
1.1. Introduction
Exchange of inJormation is a key element in international cooperation and is important
in ensuring the correct allocation of taxing rights between States and ensuring that States
can enforce their own tax laws.
The Office of the Secretary EOI at Directorate General of International Taxes, FBR, is
responsible for carrying out exchanges of information with foreign )urisdictions in
accordance with relevant provisions of international legal instruments. These
instruments include Bilateral Doubie Taxation Agreements (DTAs) of Pakistan, the
Multilateral Convention on Mutual Administrative Assistance in Tax Matters (The
Convention) and SAARC Limited Multilateral Agreement on Avoidance of Double
Taxation and Mutual Administrative Assistance on Tax Matters.
FBR believes that EOI endeavors play a strategic role for better tax administration and to
park their untaxed assets and illicit income in foreign jurisdictions particularly those
perceived as tax heavens having no or low tax rates. The EOI efforts significantly helps
Pakistan in exercising its internationai rights and obligations. Effective exchange of
information depends on the good communication and coordination between the offices
of Directorate General of International Taxes, FBR and field formations of Inland Revenue
which carry out the tax investigations in Pakistan. The office of the Secretary EOI at FBR
also contributes to administration's policies related to Exchange of Information in order
to align Pakistan's tax laws with the intemational standards on Exchange of Information.
For example, the office of the Secretary EOI FBR played a significant role to introduce the
concept of "Beneficial Owner" in a 2022 revision to the Income Tax Ordinance, 2001
Pakistan in order to address the recommendation that Pakistan received during the
OECD's Second Round Review of Pakistan's EOI by the Global Forum on transparency
and exchange of inJormation (Global Forum).
1,.2. Purpose of the Manual
The purpose of this manual is to clarify roles and responsibilities and to provide guidance
on carrying out the tasks related to EOIR and SEOI so that exchanges of information are
carried out efficiently and in accordance with internationaliy agreed standards including
the standards on foreseeable relevance and confidentiality of the information exchanged.
1.3. The brief job description of roles involved in EOI
1.3.1.
CompetentAuthority
FBR is the competent authority for Exchange of Information of Pakistan. FBR has
delegated the authority of Exchange of Information to the officers of Directorate General
of International Tax Operations FBR. The Directorate General of International Tax
5

operations approves the list of Competent Authorities for EoI, updates it as and when
there is a change and shares it with OECD's Global Forum Secretariat with the request to
update the records at their end.
The Competent Authority is responsible for the effective implementation of the
provisions of the Convention, of Pakistan's Bilateral Double Taxation Agleements and of
other international taxation instruments. In particular, the Competent Authority is
responsible for handiing and overseeing the portfolio Pakistan's EoI and sEoI and
coordinating with all information exchange partners and maintaining effective working
relationships.
Competent Authorities of Pakistan include the following positions of Federal Board oI
Revenue:
i. Chairman FBR, Islamabad/Secretary Revenue Division
ii.
Director General (International Tax Operations), FBR
iii.
The Chief International Tax Operations, FBR
iv. Secretary, Exchange of Information, FBR
The competent authoiities of FBR Pakistan are easily accessible to all the EOI partners of
Pakistan, as the list provides the email addresses and telephone numbers of the
competent authorities. In addition, the detailed profiles of the competent authorifies are
also given at FBR's website and competent authorities of FBR have regular established
contact with its EOI partners.

The field offices of Ir and Revenue shall perform the following tasks in respect of EOIRs.

Initiate a request for inJormation from a foreign tax authority by preparing an

CHAPTER 2. OUTWARD AND INWARD REQUESTS
2.7.
Types of Information
Field formations of Inland Revenue, in the course of an examination or audit, may need
information which has not been provided by the taxpayer or their agent and which does
not appear to be available within the country. The information maybe available in a
foreign jurisdiction from where the information may be requested through an outward
EOIR.
This information can help to ensure the assessments are fair and sustainable in the
appellate fora. For example, in majority of the cases when the taxPayers are confronted
with the financial accounts shared through Common Reporting standards, they tend to
deny holding those financial accounts. In such cases, the investigating tax office needs to
collect documentary evidence such as Account Opening Forms, Statement of Assets,
Banking statements, identity details of Beneficial owners etc. to reach a logical
conclusion of assessments. With this in the backdrop, the investigating tax office requests
for obtaining documentary evidence from the foreign jurisdictions. Information can also
be used to detect other cases of avoidance and evasion.
Information which is requested from a treaty partner must be "foreseeably relevant" to
the tax affairs being examined. The term "foreseeably relevant", currently used in Article
26 of the oECD Model Tax Convention is intended to provide for information to be
exchanged to the widest possible extent, but excludes "fishing expeditions" or requests
that are unlikely to be relevant to the tax affairs of a given taxpayer. It covers information
in respect of ail persons (e.g. not limited to persons that are resident in either contracting
state or nationals thereof), in both civil tax matters and criminal tax matters, such as tax
fraud and evasion. The obligation to exchange information that is "foreseeably relevant"
pursuant to a heaty is mandatory. Older treaties often use the word "necessary" for
"foreseeably relevant" and these terms are considered equivalent.
similarly, the information requested by a foreign jurisdiction tfuough an inward EoIR
may be directly available within the FBR (information on the tax return, amount of taxes
paid, etc.) or with a third party such as banks, an employer or any other organization).
Common types of information may include
o
ldentity details of owners/beneficial owners of accounts, companies, firms, trusts
etc.
o Income Tax Returns submitted to the {oreign jurisdictions and details of tax paid
abroad
o
Residence status
.
Property owned or used
o
Income and expenses

Accounting information such as Financial Accounts, balance sheets, profit and loss
accounts
.
Banking Information such as Bank account balances and transactions
e
Directors and shareholders of companies
.
Company registration and incorporations details
2.2. Outward Requests: making a request to a treaty Patrner
2.2.1. Introduction
The Regional Tax Offices (RTOs), Large Taxpayers Offices (LTOs), Directorate of
Intelligence and Investigation-Inland Revenue and other field formations of Inland
Revenue Service of FBR may need information from foreign jurisdictions to apply
Pakistan's tax laws correctly and ensure taxPayer compliance. FBR can request such
in-formation from foreign tax administrations where a legal instrument for exchange of
information, such as a DTA or the Convention or the SAARC Limited Convention is in
force.
Field offices of FBR are not allowed to request inJormation from, or exchange information
directly with, their foreign iurisdictions. Al1 requests for information from in-formation
exchange partners must only be made by the competent authority at FBR and must be
channeled tfuough the office of Secretary EOI placed at Directorate General of
International Taxes Operatioru FBR. The following procedures should be adopted in all
cases where FBR send an outward EOIR to a foreign jurisdiction for information to be
used for tax purposes.
Before making an outward EOIR, the field formations or the investigating tax offices are
expected under the terms of DTA and the convention to use all domestic means
available in Pakistan to obtain the information, except those which are 1ike1y to give rise
to disproportionate difficulties.
2.2.2. Procedures to be followed when making a request to a foreign jurisdiction
These procedures shall be followed while making an outward EOIR to foreign
jurisdictions.
2.2,2,1 Initiation of the oufiaaril EOIR.
o
EOIRs are generated by the field officers of Inland Revenue prepared on the
standard format duly signed by the concerned officer/Commissioner-Inland
Revenue on the approved formats. The EOIRs shall be generated as per the format
given in Annexure-l and wiil be accompanied by tempiate of additional
information given under Az nexure- II.
. The EOIR(s) shall be forwarded to the office of the Directorate General
International Taxes, Chief International Taxes or Secretary EOI for further process
processing and onward submission to the relevant foreign jurisdiction.
I
11

a The EOIRs may aiso be initiated by the Directorate General of International Taxes
FBR in order to obtain information for use by the field offices of Inland Revenue
in their tax investigations and audits, particularly the Automatic Exchange of
In-formation (AEOI) Zones which are under administrative authority of DG
International Tax Operations.
The EOIR(s) when received at the office of Secretary EOI, shall be marked to the
Inspector-lRs and other staff members attached with the office of the secretary EOI
for examination to ascertain if the EOIR(s) meet the standards.
2.2.2.2 Valiilating the rcquest
The inspectors-IR attached with the office of secretary (EoI) shall conduct a preliminary
examination to see if the request is valid by verifying:
o
\Atrhether an EOI arrangement exists with the requested State;
. Whether the request deais with periods or taxes which are covered by the
DTA/Convention;
o lAtrhether the request has been duly signed by the concerned officer of Inland
Revenue as per the guidance issued by the Directorate General of International
Taxes, FBR;
o
\A/hether the in{ormation requested is necessary or foreseeably relevant.
. \Alhether the information provided is sufficient and the request is clear and
specific.
r whether the investigating office of field formations have perused/exhausted all
means to obtain the requested information domestically except those which are
likely to pose difficulty of disproportionate level, before making request to foreign
jurisdiction.
o
The inspectorsJR shall be responsible for identifying discrepancies (if any) in the
EOIR(s) and draft letters to be sent to the concerned field offices to convey the
discrepancies observed.
. Once the discrepancies are addressed by the field formations and all the
information required to prepare a standard EOIR is available, the final EOIR shall
be dra{ted by the inspectors-IR and the EOIR along with covering letter shall be
presented to Secretary EOI for further examination and further processing.
o In case, it is observed that no EOI agreement exists with the jurisdiction from
which information is requested or if the request deals with periods or taxes which
are not covered by the DTA/Convention, the requesting field formation shall be
notified accordingly in writing and the case would be closed after taking approval
from Director General of International Taxes through Chief International Taxes
FBR.
2.2.2.3 Processing for approoal
.
The Assistant Private Secretary attached with the office of Secretary (EOI) shall
open a file (hard copy) for the EOIR giving a reference number to the EOIR
generated by the EOI database or allotted manually.
o
The EOIR shall be perused by the Secretary EOI. In case the Secretary EOI has any

observation or needs any clarification from field office, the matter shall again be

Annexure-Ill
.

International Tax Operations FBR along with the covering letter addressed to the

attachments, if any, for perusal of the Chief International Taxes and approval.
Model template of the cot eing letter Annexure-IV.
a The checklist of what is to be included in outward EOIR is attached at Annexure-
v.

a The Chief International Taxes shall sign the EOIR and the covering letter and

kansmission of the request to the concerned foreign jurisdiction.
The office of the Secretary EOI shall transmit the EOIR along with covering letter

a The inspectors-IR attached with the office of the Secretary (EOI) shall enter the

reports, quoting the reference number:
/ alter 30 days, if an acknowledgement of the request has not been
received; or
/
alter 90 days, il the information or a status rePort on the request is not

/
at regular intervals thereafter, depending on the circumstances.
a The inspectorslR attached with the office of the Secretary (EOI) shali update the

2.2.2.4 Receiaing response to an outward EOIR
Following actions shall be taken when information/documents are received at the
Directorate General of International Taxes in response to Pakistan's outward EOIR.
In case the response is received through emails, the APS attached with the office
of Secretary EOI shall retrieve the documents and print all the documents
received. The AI5 affixes the stamp of "Confidentiality" on each page of the
documents and present the documents to Secretary EOI for perusal and directions
if any.
a The Secretary EOI shall mark the documents to the inspector-IR or AI5 for
examination if the inIormation provided are complete as per the information
requested.
a In case there is any shortcoming or shortfall in the documents the matter shall be
taken up with the concerned foreign jurisdiction through email or letter requesting
them to provide the missing documents. The letter shall also acknowledge the
receipt of the documents.
a In case the information/documents are complete and no shortfall is observed the
letter/email of acknowledgment shall be sent to the supplying state.
a The Secretary EOI) after taking approval, shall share the information received
from foreign iurisdiction to the concerned tax office for use in investigation. The
letter sent in this regard, shall also request the field office to provide feedback on
usefulness and utility of information provided. This letter sha1l also advise the
concerned tax office to keep confidentiality of the documents shared. Model
template of the letter sharing information ruith field office is attachtd at Annexure'W.
2.3.
Inward Requests: receiving a request from a treaty Partner
2.3.L. Introduction
The Directorate General of International Tax Operations, FBR aims to meet the highest
intemational standards in EOI for tax purposes. For this reasor! in-formation requests
shall be dealt with as quickly as possible and clear timelines shall be established within
which replies are to be given to EOI requests received from foreign jurisdictions.
The process of responding to a request for exchange of information has been divided into
different steps described below.
-14

2.3.1.L Logging the request
a Inward EOIR shall be considered to have been received at FBR on the date when
it is received in the office of Secretary EOI through email or registered post. The
EOIR shall be marked to the concerned inspector-IR with the advice to examine
and take necessary action.
The inspectors-IR shall enter the details of the EOIR in the EOI Database System
i.e. case name, date the case was received, foreign reference number, requesting
state and include details of the inlormation request.
The acknowledgement of receipt of the EOIR shall generally be sent through email
while requesting the password for the encrypted files.
2.3.1.2 Validating the request
a A11 requests shall initially be examined by inspectors-IR attached with the office
of Secretary EOI. The clarification questions would be raised or the request will be
returned to the requesting State if:
/
it is not covered by an international tax treaty; or
/
it deals with periods, or taxes, which are not covered by the international
tax keaty; or

CHAPTER 5 - SPONTANEOUS EOI
5.1. Introduction
The Multiiateral Convention on Mutual Administrative Assistance on Tax Matters
provides for the Spontaneous Exchange of Information. As a signatory to the Convention
i,akistan encourages Spontaneous Exchange of Information with Pakistan's information
exchange partneri. Spontaneous Exchange of Information are valuable for both Pakistan
and the foreign jurisdiction in uncovering specific cases of tax evasion. Directorate
General of International Taxes FBR urges the field formation of Inland Revenue to
generate Spontaneous Exchange of InJormation whenever the investigating tax office
io-"" u.roir details of income, assets or a transaction that is likely to be very useful for
a tax authority of foreign jurisdiction.
Spontaneous exchange may occur where:
. There are grounds for suspecting that there may be a significant tax loss by the
foreign jurisdiction.
.
A person liable to tax obtains a reduction in, or an exemption from tax in one State
which would give rise to an increase in tax or to liability to tax in the other State.
o Business dealings between a person liable to tax in one State and a person liable to
tax in the other State are conducted tfuough one or more States in such a way that
a loss of tax may result in one of the States, or in both.
. There are grounds for supposing that a loss of tax mav resuit from artificial
transfers of profits within groups of enterprises.

CHAPTER 6 - CONFIDENTIALITY
6.1. Introduction
Taxpayer inJormation exchanged under EOI and Spontaneous Exchange of
Information is kept strictly confidentiai under Article 22 (Secrecy) of Multilateral
Convention on Mutual Administrative Assistance in tax matters and under the
"Exchange of In{ormation" Article of Pakistan's Double Taxation Agreements.
As a signatory to the Multilateral Convention on Mutual Administrative Assistance
in tax matters and Pakistan DTAs, FBR maintains complete confidentiality of
information exchanged under the Multilateral Convention on Mutual
Administrative Assistance in tax matters and Pakistan DTAs.
The provisions shall apply to information received in response to an outward EOIR,
to in{ormation being exchanged as an inward EOIR, to Spontaneous Exchange of
InJormation and also to in-formation contained in correspondence between
competent authorities, including emails and letters written by the competent
authorities. At the same time, it is understood that a requested State can disclose the
minimum inlormation necessary contained in a competent authority letter (but not
the letter itself) for the requested State to be able to obtain the requested information
and provide it to the requesting State. \4rhen requesting information from third
parties or other government agencies, only the information needed to take action
on the request shall be provided.
In{ormation, received under the provisions of a freaty and the Convention, shall be
treated as secret in the same manner as information obtained under domestic law
and in line with the requirements of the treaty and the provisions of the Convention.
Fiscal statute of Pakistan also provides for confidentiality of information thus
obtained or exchanged. In Income Tax Ordinance, 2001., for instance, under
subsection 18 of section 1,07 any information received or supplied, and any
concomitant communications or correspondences made with governments of
foreign countries under a tax treaty, a tax information exchange agreement, a
multilateral convention, a similar arrangement or mechanism shail be kept
confidential. Similarly, section 564., 568 of Sales Tax Act, 1990 and sections 47A, tlTB
of Federal Excise Act, 2005 covers the issue of confidentiality of such information
and data received or supplied.
